blocks:
Ayahs

إِذَا ٱلشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ (١)
When the sun is wound up, (1)
وَإِذَا ٱلنُّجُومُ ٱنكَدَرَتْ (٢)
when the stars scatter, (2)
وَإِذَا ٱلْجِبَالُ سُيِّرَتْ (٣)
when the mountains are set moving, (3)
وَإِذَا ٱلْعِشَارُ عُطِّلَتْ (٤)
when the pregnant camels are neglected, (4)
وَإِذَا ٱلْوُحُوشُ حُشِرَتْ (٥)
when the wild beasts are mustered, (5)
وَإِذَا ٱلْبِحَارُ سُجِّرَتْ (٦)
when the seas are set afire, (6)
وَإِذَا ٱلنُّفُوسُ زُوِّجَتْ (٧)
when the souls are assorted, (7)
وَإِذَا ٱلْمَوْءُۥدَةُ سُئِلَتْ (٨)
when the girl buried-alive will be asked (8)
بِأَىِّ ذَنۢبٍ قُتِلَتْ (٩)
for what sin she was killed. (9)

Content of Surah At-Takwir

This Surah is among the Meccan Surahs, and various evidences attest to this fact. Among them is the indication that the stubborn enemies used to attribute madness to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). This sentiment was typically prevalent in Mecca, especially at the beginning of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mission, when the enemies sought to dismiss his words and ignore them with indifference.

In any case, this Surah revolves primarily around two axes:

The first axis consists of the opening verses of this Surah, which describe the signs of the Day of Resurrection and the immense transformations at the end of this world and the beginning of the Rising.

The second axis speaks of the greatness of the Quran, its bringer, and its impact on human souls; this section is accompanied by awakening and profound oaths.

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

Numerous hadiths have been narrated regarding the importance of this Surah and its recitation. Among them, we read in a hadith from the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله):

مَنْ قَرَأَ سُورَةَ إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ أَعَاذَهُ اللَّهُ تَعَالَى أَنْ يَفْضَحَهُ حِينَ تُنْشَرُ صَحِيفَتُهُ

"Whoever recites Surah 'When the sun is folded up' [At-Takwir], Allah the Exalted will protect him from scandal at the time when the records of deeds are unfolded." [^1] In another hadith narrated from the same Prophet, he said:

مَنْ أَحَبَّ أَنْ يَنْظُرَ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ فَلْيَقْرَأْ إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ

"Whoever wishes to look at me on the Day of Resurrection should recite Surah 'When the sun is folded up'." [^2] This hadith has also been narrated in another form:

مَنْ سَرَّهُ أَنْ يَنْظُرَ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ (كَأَنَّهُ رَأْيُ عَيْنٍ) فَلْيَقْرَأْ "إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ" وَ "إِذَا السَّمَاءُ انْفَطَرَتْ" وَ "إِذَا السَّمَاءُ انْشَقَّتْ"

"Whoever is pleased to behold the Resurrection (as if seeing it with his own eyes) should recite Surah 'When the sun is folded up', Surah 'When the heaven is cleft asunder', and Surah 'When the heaven is split asunder'." (Because in these Surahs, the signs of the Resurrection are described in such a way that it places the reciter as if before the scene of the Resurrection). [^3] In another hadith, we read that the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) was asked: "Why have the signs of old age appeared in you so early?" He replied:

شَيَّبَتْنِي هُودٌ وَ الْوَاقِعَةُ وَ الْمُرْسَلَاتُ وَ عَمَّ يَتَسَاءَلُونَ وَ إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ

"Surah Hud, Al-Waqi'ah, Al-Mursalat, 'Amma Yatasa'alun, and 'When the sun is folded up' have made me old." (Because the terrifying events of the Resurrection are depicted in them in such a way that they cause premature aging in any awakened person). [^4] In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), it is stated: "Whoever recites Surah 'He frowned and turned away' [Abasa] and 'When the sun is folded up' [At-Takwir], he will be under the wing of the Lord's grace in the eternal Paradise, and under the shadow of Divine grace and generosity in the gardens of Heaven, and this is not a difficult thing for Allah if He wills." [^1] The expressions used in the above narrations clearly show that the intended recitation is one that is the source of awareness, faith, and action.

[^1]: and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 441. [^3]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 7017; the meaning of this hadith is also probable for the previous hadith. [^4]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 513. [^1]: Thawab al-A'mal as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 512.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ

إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ (1) وَ إِذَا النُّجُومُ انْكَدَرَتْ (2) وَ إِذَا الْجِبالُ سُيِّرَتْ (3) وَ إِذَا الْعِشارُ عُطِّلَتْ (4)

وَ إِذَا الْوُحُوشُ حُشِرَتْ (5) وَ إِذَا الْبِحارُ سُجِّرَتْ (6) وَ إِذَا النُّفُوسُ زُوِّجَتْ (7) وَ إِذَا الْمَوْؤُدَةُ سُئِلَتْ (8) بِأَيِّ ذَنْبٍ قُتِلَتْ (9)

Commentary

The Day the Scroll of the Universe is Folded

At the beginning of this Surah, as we mentioned, we encounter short, exciting, and shocking references to the terrifying events of the end of this world and the beginning of the Resurrection, which take man through extraordinary realms. In total, it recounts eight of these signs.

First, He says: "When the sun is folded up" (إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ).

"Kuwwirat" is from the root "takwir," which originally means to wrap or fold something up (like wrapping a turban on the head). This is what is understood from various books of linguistics and exegesis. Sometimes it is said to mean "to cast down" or "to become dark," and it seems that these two meanings also return to the same primary root.

In any case, what is meant here is the folding up of the sun's light, its darkening, and the shrinking of its volume.

We know that the sun is currently an extraordinarily hot and burning sphere, to the extent that all its matter has turned into compressed gas, and around it, burning flames flare up reaching heights of hundreds of thousands of kilometers! If the Earth were caught in the middle of one of these massive flames, it would instantly turn to ash and a handful of gas! But at the end of this world and on the threshold of the Resurrection, this heat will subside, those flames will be gathered, its light will tend toward extinction, and its volume will decrease; this is the meaning of "takwir." Thus, it is stated in Lisan al-Arab: "Kuwwirat al-shams: its light is gathered and wrapped just as a turban is wrapped."

This is a reality reflected in modern science as well; it is established that the sun is gradually moving toward darkness and extinction.

Then He adds: "And when the stars lose their light and fall" (وَ إِذَا النُّجُومُ انْكَدَرَتْ). "Inkadarat" is from the root "inkidar," meaning "to fall and be scattered," and it is from the root "kudurat," meaning "dimness and darkness." Combining both meanings in the verse under discussion is possible, because on the threshold of the Resurrection, the stars will both lose their brilliance and light, and they will be scattered and fall, and the system of the upper world will be disrupted, just as stated in verse 2 of Surah Al-Infitar: "When the stars are scattered," and as stated in verse 8 of Surah Al-Mursalat: "When the stars are extinguished."

In the third sign of the Resurrection, He says: "And when the mountains are set in motion" (وَ إِذَا الْجِبالُ سُيِّرَتْ). As we have previously pointed out, it is understood from various verses of the Quran that on the threshold of the Resurrection, the mountains will pass through different stages: first, they are set in motion, and in the final stage, they turn into scattered dust (for more details on this, see the commentary on verse 20 of Surah An-Naba in this same volume).

Then He adds: "And when the most precious properties are abandoned" (وَ إِذَا الْعِشارُ عُطِّلَتْ). "Ishar" is the plural of "ushara," which originally means a pregnant she-camel that has passed ten months of its term and is on the verge of giving birth; meaning it won't be long before another camel is born from it, and abundant milk appears in its udders. On the day these verses were revealed, such a camel was considered the most valuable property of the Arabs.

"Uttilat" is from the root "ta'til," meaning to leave something without a guardian or shepherd.

The meaning is that the intensity of the horror and terror of that day is such that every human will forget his most precious possessions.

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan narrates that some have taken "ishar" to mean "clouds," and "uttilat" to mean the cessation of their rain; meaning on that day, clouds appear in the sky but do not rain (it is possible these clouds result from various gases or atomic clouds or dust clouds from the disintegration of mountains on the threshold of the Resurrection, which are clouds without rain).

However, Tabarsi adds: "Some have said that interpreting 'ishar' as 'clouds' is something not known in the Arabic language."

But considering what Turayhi mentioned in Majma' al-Bahrayn—that "ishar" originally means pregnant camels and was then applied to any pregnant being—it is possible its application to "clouds" is because they are also often "pregnant" [with rain], even though the clouds appearing on the threshold of the Resurrection in the sky are not pregnant (reflect on this).

Some have also interpreted "ishar" as houses or agricultural lands that will be abandoned on the threshold of the Resurrection and emptied of inhabitants and crops.

However, the first interpretation is the most well-known of all.

In the next verse, He adds: "And when the wild beasts are gathered" (وَ إِذَا الْوُحُوشُ حُشِرَتْ). The very animals that in normal circumstances were far from each other, feared one another, and fled, but the intensity of the terror of the horrific events on the threshold of the Resurrection is such that it gathers them together, and they forget everything, as if they want to reduce their fear and terror through this gathering.

In other words: when those terrifying scenes strip the wild beasts of their specific characteristics, what will they do to humans?! However, many commentators believe that the above verse refers to the "gathering of wild beasts" in the court of the Resurrection, as they too have responsibilities within their own world and the limits of their awareness, and if they have committed oppression against one another, retribution will be taken there. They consider this verse similar to verse 38 of Surah Al-An'am, which says: "There is no creature on the earth nor a bird that flies with its two wings except that they are communities like you. We have not neglected anything in the Book, then unto their Lord they shall be gathered."

Regarding the gathering and accounting of animals, we had a detailed discussion under that same verse in Surah Al-An'am (Vol. 5, pp. 224 to 227), but what can be said here is that given that the verses under discussion speak of the terrifying signs of the end of the world and the beginning of the Hereafter, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Then He adds: "And when the seas are set on fire!" (وَ إِذَا الْبِحارُ سُجِّرَتْ). "Sujjirat" is from the root "tasjir," which originally means to kindle and stir up a fire.

If this Quranic expression was strange to commentators in the past, it is not a cause for wonder for us today, because we know that water is composed of two substances, "oxygen" and "hydrogen," both of which are highly flammable. It is not unlikely that on the threshold of the Resurrection, the water of the seas will be under such pressure that they decompose and turn into a single mass of fire.

Some have interpreted this word to mean "to be filled," just as a furnace full of fire is called "musajjar." It is possible that the earthquakes of the threshold of the Resurrection and the disintegration of mountains will cause the seas to be filled, or celestial stones will fall into them and fill them, and their turbulent waters will flow over the surface of the lands and drown everything.

Then He adds: "And when the souls are paired" (وَ إِذَا النُّفُوسُ زُوِّجَتْ). The righteous with the righteous, and the wicked with the wicked; the Companions of the Right with the Companions of the Right, and the Companions of the Left with the Companions of the Left. Unlike this world where everyone is mixed—sometimes a believer's neighbor is a polytheist, and sometimes a righteous spouse has an unrighteous partner—in the Resurrection, which is the Day of Sorting and the day of separations, these ranks will be completely separated from each other.

In the interpretation of this verse, other possibilities have also been mentioned, including:

The souls return to the bodies, or the heavenly souls are paired with the Houris and the hellish souls are joined with the devils, or that every human is joined with his friend and companion after death had separated them, or every human is joined with his deeds.

However, the first interpretation seems the most appropriate of all, and verses 7 to 11 of Surah Al-Waqi'ah testify to it: "And you become three groups: then the Companions of the Right—what are the Companions of the Right? And the Companions of the Left—what are the Companions of the Left? And the Foremost are the Foremost; those are the ones brought near."

In fact, after mentioning six great transformations that are precursors to the Resurrection, this verse points to the first dawn of that great day, which is the day when everyone is accompanied by their peer.

Then, moving to another event of the Resurrection, He adds: "And when the girl-child buried alive is asked" (وَ إِذَا الْمَوْؤُدَةُ سُئِلَتْ).

"For what sin she was killed?" (بِأَيِّ ذَنْبٍ قُتِلَتْ). "Maw'udah" is from the root "wa'd," meaning a girl who is buried alive under the earth. Some have said its root means weight and heaviness, and because these girls are buried in the grave and earth is poured over them, this expression is used for them.

In some narrations, the interpretation of this verse has been expanded to include any kind of severing of kinship ties or the severing of the affection (mawaddah) for the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام).

In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), we read that when he was asked about the interpretation of this verse, he said: "It refers to those who are killed in the path of our love and affection." [^1] In another hadith, it is stated that the evidence for this is the Verse of Nearness (al-Qurba): "Say: I do not ask you for any reward for it except affection for my near relatives." [^2] Of course, the apparent meaning of the verse is the first interpretation, but its criterion and concept are capable of such expansion.

Key Points

1. Burying Girls Alive:

One of the most painful and barbaric phenomena of the era of Arab Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) was the phenomenon of "wa'd," which is repeatedly mentioned in the Holy Quran.

Although some believe that this crime was not universal among all Arab tribes and was only in the tribe of "Kindah" (or some other tribes), it certainly was not considered a rare matter; otherwise, the Quran would not have spoken about it with such emphasis and repeatedly.

In any case, this act is so horrific that even rare instances of it are worthy of attention and analysis.

Commentators have said: In the Arab Jahiliyyah, when the time for a woman's delivery arrived, she would dig a hole in the ground and sit over it. If the newborn was a girl, she would throw it into the hole, and if it was a boy, she would keep it. Thus, one of their poets says on this subject in a boastful tone:

I named her 'Tamut' (she dies) when she was born, And the grave is a son-in-law who has embraced and silenced her! [^1]

The factors behind the emergence of this crime were various, including:

The lack of value for women as human beings in the Jahili society.

The issue of extreme poverty that governed that society, especially considering that girls, unlike boys, were not capable of economic production and did not participate in raids.

Also, the thought that in the frequent tribal wars of that day, girls might be taken captive and their "honor" would fall into the hands of strangers, thereby bringing a stain of disgrace upon them, was not without effect in this practice.

These multiple factors joined hands and created the horrific phenomenon of "wa'd" and burying girls alive.

With great regret, this issue manifests in other forms in the ignorance of recent centuries, in the form of free abortion which has become legally prevalent in many so-called civilized countries. If the Jahili Arab killed newborns after birth, the civilized humans of our era kill them in the mother's womb! We have written a more detailed explanation in this regard in Vol. 11, pp. 270 to 274 (under verse 59 of Surah An-Nahl).

  1. It is noteworthy that the Holy Quran has considered this issue so ugly and loathsome, and has dealt with it so decisively, that it even places the investigation of this matter before the issue of publishing the records of deeds in the Resurrection and seeking justice in other matters. This shows the ultimate importance Islam places on human blood, especially innocent humans, as well as the value of the female gender from the perspective of Islam.
  1. Another point necessary to note here is that the Quran does not say they will ask the killers; rather, it says these innocent children will be asked what their sin was that they were so mercilessly killed? It is as if the killers are not even worth interrogating; furthermore, the testimony and witness of these victims alone is sufficient.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا ٱلصُّحُفُ نُشِرَتْ (١٠)
When the records [of deeds] are unfolded, (10)
وَإِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ كُشِطَتْ (١١)
when the sky is stripped off, (11)
وَإِذَا ٱلْجَحِيمُ سُعِّرَتْ (١٢)
when hell is set ablaze, (12)
وَإِذَا ٱلْجَنَّةُ أُزْلِفَتْ (١٣)
when paradise is brought near, (13)
عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ مَّآ أَحْضَرَتْ (١٤)
then a soul shall know what it has readied [for itself]. (14)

Commentary

On That Day, All Shall Know What They Have Done

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the first stage of the Resurrection## Commentary

On That Day, All Shall Know What They Have Done

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the first stage of the Resurrection—namely, the stage of this world's destruction—the verses under discussion point to its second stage: the emergence and manifestation of the other world and the auditing of deeds.

First, It states: "And when the records [of deeds] are laid open" (وَ إِذَا الصُّحُفُ نُشِرَتْ‌).

"Suhuf" (صُحُف) is the plural of "Sahifah" (صَحيفَة), which originally means something spread out, like the surface of the face, and was subsequently applied to tablets and papers upon which matters are written.

The opening of the records of deeds on the Day of Judgment occurs before the eyes of their owners, so they may read them and settle their own accounts, as stated in Surah Al-Isra, verse 14: "Read your book; sufficient is your soul this day as an accountant against you" (اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ كَفى‌ بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيباً).

It also occurs before the eyes of others, which serves as an encouragement for the righteous and a punishment and source of anguish for the wicked.

Then It adds: "And when the sky is stripped away" (وَ إِذَا السَّماءُ كُشِطَتْ‌). "Kushitat" (كُشِطَتْ) is derived from the root "Kashat" (كشط)—rhyming with "Kashf"—which originally, as Raghib states in Al-Mufradat, means "to skin an animal." According to Ibn Manzur in Lisan al-Arab, it also means to remove a veil or covering from something; hence, this expression is used when clouds are torn apart and dispersed.

The meaning in the verse under discussion is that the veils cast over the material world and the celestial realm in this life—which prevent people from seeing the angels, Paradise, or Hell that exist within this universe—will be cast aside. Human beings will then behold the realities of existence, and as mentioned in the subsequent verses, Hell will be set ablaze and Paradise will be brought near to mankind.

Indeed, the Day of Resurrection is the "Day of Manifestation" (Yawn al-Buruz), and the realities of things will become apparent and clear on that day, and the veil will be lifted from the sky.

According to this interpretation, the above verse refers to the events of the second stage of the Resurrection—the stage of humanity's return to life and a new existence. The preceding and succeeding verses also point to this meaning. The view held by many commentators—that it refers to the folding of the heavens and the events of the first stage of Resurrection (the annihilation of the world)—seems very unlikely. This is because it neither aligns with the root meaning of "Kushitat" (skinning or removing a veil) nor is it consistent with the sequence of the preceding and following verses.

Therefore, in the next verse It adds: "And when Hellfire is set ablaze" (وَ إِذَا الْجَحِيمُ سُعِّرَتْ‌). According to the verse: "And indeed, Hell encompasses the disbelievers" (وَ إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ - At-Tawbah: 49), Hell exists even today, but the veils and barriers of the worldly realm prevent its observation. Similarly, according to many Quranic verses, Paradise is also currently prepared for the God-fearing [1].

For this same reason, the next verse states: "And when Paradise is brought near" (وَ إِذَا الْجَنَّةُ أُزْلِفَتْ‌). The same meaning appears in verse 90 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, with the difference that the "God-fearing" (Muttaqin) are explicitly mentioned there, while they are implied here.

"Uzlifat" (أُزْلِفَتْ) is derived from the root "Zalf" (rhyming with "Harf") and "Zulfa" (rhyming with "Kubra"), meaning proximity and nearness. This could refer to proximity in space, time, or in terms of means and preliminaries—or all of these. That is, Paradise becomes near to the believers in terms of location, the time of entry is imminent, and the means to reach it are made easy.

It is noteworthy that It does not say "the righteous draw near to Paradise," but rather "Paradise is brought near to them," which is the most honorable expression possible in this context.

As we have stated, Paradise and Hell exist now, but on that day, Paradise will be brought closer and Hell will be more ablaze than ever.

Finally, in the last verse under discussion—which is effectively the conclusion of all the preceding verses and the "result" (jaza') for the conditional statements (shart) mentioned in the twelve previous verses—It states: "Every soul shall know what it has brought [with it]"! (عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ ما أَحْضَرَتْ‌).

This expression clearly demonstrates that all human deeds will be present there, and man's knowledge and awareness of them will be a knowledge accompanied by witnessing and observation.

This reality is mentioned in several other verses of the Quran. In Surah Al-Kahf, verse 49, we read: "And they will find what they did present" (وَ وَجَدُوا ما عَمِلُوا حاضِراً). And in the final verses of Surah Az-Zalzalah: "So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it" (فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ‌).

This verse also speaks to the "embodiment of deeds" (tajassum al-a'mal)—the fact that human actions, which seemingly perish in this world, are never truly destroyed. On that day, they will be embodied in appropriate forms and will be present in the arena of the Gathering.

Key Points

1. The Order of the Verses

In the verses under discussion and the preceding ones, twelve events related to the Resurrection are mentioned. The first six events pertain to the first stage—the annihilation of this world—and the second six events pertain to the second stage—the resurrection and life after death. In the first part, the talk is of the darkening of the sun, the loss of brilliance of the stars, the shaking and movement of mountains, the ignition of the seas, the forgetting of wealth and property, and the terror of wild animals.

In the second stage, the talk is of the gathering of humans in separate ranks, the questioning regarding the innocent girls buried alive, the opening of the records of deeds, the removal of veils from the face of the sky, the blazing of the fire of Hell, the drawing near of Paradise, and finally, man's complete awareness of his own deeds.

These verses, while brief, are so meaningful, resonant, and awakening that they shake every human being and plunge them into a world of reflection. It is as if one sees the end of this world and the nature of the Resurrection manifested before their eyes in short phrases. How beautiful and eloquent are the verses of the Quran and its expressions, and how meaningful and inspiring are its indications and nuances!

2. Will the Solar System and the Stars Be Extinguished?

Before anything else, we must know that the sun—this life-giving center of our system—though a medium-sized star compared to the stars of the heavens, is extraordinarily vast in its own right and in relation to the Earth. According to scientific research, its volume is 1,300,000 times larger than the Earth! However, because it is approximately 150 million kilometers away from us, it appears at its current size.

To visualize the magnitude and scale of the sun, it is enough to say that if the Moon and the Earth were placed inside the sun with the same distance that currently exists between them, the Moon could easily orbit the Earth without ever leaving the sun's surface! The surface temperature of the sun is estimated at 6,000 degrees Celsius, and the temperature at its core is estimated at several million degrees!!! If we wanted to express the weight of the sun in tons, we would have to write the number "2" followed by twenty-seven zeros (i.e., two billion billion billion tons)! Flames leap from the sun's surface, sometimes reaching heights of 160,000 kilometers, in which the Earth would easily be lost, as the Earth's diameter is just over 12,000 kilometers.

As for the source of the sun's thermal and light energy, contrary to what some imagine, it does not result from combustion. As George Gamow states in his book The Birth and Death of the Sun, if the sun's mass were made of pure coal and had been set on fire during the time of the first Egyptian Pharaoh, it would have been entirely consumed by today, leaving nothing but ash. Any other combustible material assumed in place of coal would face the same problem.

The truth is that the concept of "burning" does not apply to the sun. What applies is the energy resulting from atomic disintegration. We know this energy is extraordinarily vast. Therefore, the sun's atoms are constantly undergoing disintegration, radiation, and conversion into energy. According to scientific calculations, every second that passes, its mass decreases by four million tons! However, the sun's volume is so immense that even after thousands of years, it shows no sign of strain, and no apparent change occurs in its status.

However, it must be understood that this very process contributes to the eventual annihilation and destruction of the sun in the long run. Eventually, this massive body will become thinner and thinner, its light will fade, and it will ultimately become dark. This also applies to other stars [1]. Therefore, what is mentioned in the above verses regarding the darkening of the sun and the disintegration of the stars is a reality that is completely consistent with modern science. The Quran expressed these truths at a time when there was no news of such matters, not only in the environment of the Arabian Peninsula but even in the scientific circles of the world at that time.

[^1]: Adapted from the books The Birth and Death of the Sun, Astronomy Without a Telescope, and The Structure of the Sun.

Ayahs

فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلْخُنَّسِ (١٥)
So I swear by the stars that return, (15)
ٱلْجَوَارِ ٱلْكُنَّسِ (١٦)
the comets, (16)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا عَسْعَسَ (١٧)
by the night as it approaches, (17)
وَٱلصُّبْحِ إِذَا تَنَفَّسَ (١٨)
by the dawn as it breathes: (18)
إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (١٩)
it is indeed the speech of a noble apostle, (19)
ذِى قُوَّةٍ عِندَ ذِى ٱلْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ (٢٠)
powerful and eminent with the Lord of the Throne, (20)
مُّطَاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ (٢١)
one who is obeyed and is trustworthy as well. (21)
وَمَا صَاحِبُكُم بِمَجْنُونٍ (٢٢)
Your companion is not crazy: (22)
وَلَقَدْ رَءَاهُ بِٱلْأُفُقِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢٣)
certainly he saw him on the manifest horizon, (23)
وَمَا هُوَ عَلَى ٱلْغَيْبِ بِضَنِينٍ (٢٤)
and he is not miserly concerning the Unseen. (24)
وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَيْطَـٰنٍ رَّجِيمٍ (٢٥)
And it is not the speech of an outcast Satan. (25)

Commentary

The Messenger of Divine Revelation Has Descended Upon Him

Following the previous verses which spoke of the Resurrection, the precursors to the Day of Judgment, and some of the events of that Great Day, these verses discuss the truthfulness of the Quran and the veracity of the words of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). In reality, they emphasize what was mentioned in the preceding verses regarding the Resurrection and reinforce the matter by mentioning enlightening oaths.

First, He says: "So I swear by the stars that retreat," (فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِالْخُنَّسِ) [1]

[1] Regarding whether the "la" (لا) in "la uqsimu" (لا أقسم) is a negative particle or an augmentative one for emphasis, commentators have various discussions here. Since we discussed this at the beginning of Surah Al-Qiyamah in this same volume, there is no need for repetition.

"That move rapidly and hide themselves," (الْجَوارِ الْكُنَّسِ).

"Khunnas" (خنس) is the plural of "khanis" (خانس), from the root "khunus" (خنس), which originally means contraction, returning, and hiding. Satan is called "khannas" (خناس) for this reason—because he hides himself and retreats when the name of Allah is mentioned, as stated in the hadith:

الشيطان يوسوس الى العبد فاذا ذكر اللَّه خنس

"Satan continuously whispers to the servants of Allah, and when they remember Allah, he retreats." [1]

"Jawar" (جوار) is the plural of "jariyah" (جاريه), meaning a swift mover.

"Kunnas" (كنس) is the plural of "kanis" (كانس), from the root "kunus" (كنس), meaning to hide. "Kinas" (كناس) refers to the nests of birds and the hiding places of deer and wild animals.

Regarding what is meant by these oaths, many commentators believe they refer to the five planets of the solar system visible to the naked eye (Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn).

To explain: if we fix our eyes on the sky for several consecutive nights, we realize that the stars of the sky generally rise gradually and set together without any change in the distances between them. It is as if they are pearls sewn onto a black cloth at specific intervals, and this cloth is pulled up from one side and down from the other. Only five stars are exceptions to this general rule; that is, they move amidst the other stars as if they were five unsewn pearls placed freely on this cloth, rolling among them! These are the same five stars mentioned above, which are members of the solar system family. Their movements are apparent due to their proximity to us; otherwise, all other stars in the sky also possess such movements, but because they are so far from us, we cannot perceive their motion. This is one aspect.

On the other hand, it is necessary to note that astronomers have called these stars "wandering stars" (نجوم متحيره) because their movements are not in a straight line. It appears that they travel for a while, then return slightly, and then continue their journey again—the causes of which have been extensively discussed in the science of astronomy.

The above verses may refer to the fact that these stars possess motion (al-jawar), and in their path, they have a return or retrograde motion (al-khunnas), and finally, at the break of dawn and sunrise, they become hidden and concealed (al-kunnas), similar to deer that roam the wilderness at night to find food and hide in their dens (kinas) during the day for fear of hunters and wild animals.

There is also the possibility that "kunnas" refers to their disappearance in the sun's rays. This means that while orbiting the sun, they sometimes reach a point where they are positioned beside the sun and are absolutely invisible, which astronomers refer to as "combustion" (ihtiraq). This is a subtle point that becomes clear upon careful observation of the state of these planets.

Some also consider "kunnas" to refer to these stars entering the zodiac signs, which resembles deer hiding in their nests. Of course, it is clear that the planets of the solar system are not limited to these five; three other planets

[1] Lisan al-Arab, root kh-n-s.

named Uranus, Pluto, and Neptune exist, which are only observable with astronomical telescopes. Together with Earth, they form the nine planets of the solar system (though many of these nine planets have "moons," which are separate from this count). Additionally, the term "jawari" (جوارى), the plural of "jariyah," one of whose meanings is "moving ships," is a beautiful expression showing the similarity of the movement of these stars in the ocean of the sky to the movement of ships on the surface of the seas.

In any case, it seems the Holy Quran intends, through these meaningful oaths tinged with a kind of ambiguity, to set thoughts in motion and draw attention to the unique and exceptional state of these planets among the vast host of stars in the sky, so that people may reflect more upon them and become better acquainted with the greatness of the Creator of this magnificent system.

Some commentators have provided other interpretations for the above verses, which were omitted as they were not significant.

In a hadith from Amir al-Mu'minin (عليه السلام), it is stated regarding the interpretation of these verses:

هى خمسة انجم: زحل، و المشترى و المريخ، و الزهره، و عطارد

"They are five stars: Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Venus, and Mercury." [1]

Then He proceeds to the second oath, saying: "And by the night as it retreats and comes to an end," (وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا عَسْعَسَ).

"As'asa" (عسعس) is from the root "as'asah" (عسعسة), which originally means thin darkness. Since darkness is thinner at the beginning and end of the night, this term is used for both the approaching and the retreating of the night. The term "asas" (عسس) for night watchmen is also related to this.

Although this word, as mentioned, has two completely different meanings, here, by the context of the following verse which speaks of the morning, it refers to the ending of the night. In reality, it is similar to the oath in verse 33 of Surah Al-Muddaththir: وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذْ أَدْبَرَ ("And by the night when it retreats").

Fundamentally, the night—as previously mentioned—is one of the great divine blessings; it is a source of tranquility for the soul and body, a means of moderating the sun's heat, and essential for the continuation of life. However, the emphasis on the end of the night may be because it moves toward light and illumination, and furthermore, it is the best time for supplication and worship of the Lord, and the beginning of movement and activity in the world of life.

And finally, He turns to the third and last oath, saying: "And by the morning when it breathes!" (وَ الصُّبْحِ إِذا تَنَفَّسَ).

What a beautiful expression! The morning is likened to a living being whose first breath begins with the rising of the dawn, breathing the spirit of life into all creatures. It is as if its breath had been cut off under the feet of the dark army of night, and with the shining of the first ray of light, it is freed from those claws and takes a fresh breath!

This expression is similar to the one in Surah Al-Muddaththir following the oath by the night, where it says: وَ الصُّبْحِ إِذا أَسْفَرَ ("And by the morning when it brightens/unveils its face"). It is as if the darkness of night was like a black veil over the face of the morning; at dawn, it casts aside the veil to show its luminous and radiant face—the sign of life—to the worlds.

In the following verse, He addresses the subject for which all these oaths were taken, saying: "Indeed, this [Quran] is the word of a noble messenger (Gabriel the Trustworthy) who brought it from Allah to His Prophet," (إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ).

This is a response to those who accused the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) of fabricating the Quran himself and attributing it to Allah.

In this verse and the following ones, five attributes are mentioned for "Gabriel," the messenger of Allah's revelation, which are, in fact, attributes necessary for any qualified messenger.

First is his description as "noble" (karim), which refers to his existential value. Yes, he is a being of value and importance in the sight of the Great Allah.

Then, proceeding to his other attributes, He adds: "Possessed of power and high rank with the Lord of the Throne," (ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ). [1]

"Lord of the Throne" (Dhi al-Arsh) refers to the Pure Essence of Allah. Although He is the Lord of the entire universe, since the Throne—whether it means the metaphysical world or the station of Allah's hidden knowledge—holds greater importance, He describes Him as the Lord of the Throne.

The description "possessed of power" (dhi quwwah) regarding Gabriel is because receiving such a great message and delivering it accurately requires immense power and strength. Fundamentally, every messenger must possess power within the scope of his mission; specifically, he must be free from any forgetfulness regarding the message he is responsible for delivering.

"Makin" (مكين) means one who possesses status and "rank" (makanat). Essentially, the messenger of a great person must be an outstanding individual capable of undertaking that representation and mission, and must be completely close and near to him. Certainly, the expression "with" ('inda) does not mean physical presence, for Allah has no physical location; rather, it refers to a presence of status and spiritual proximity.

In the fourth and fifth descriptions, He says: "Obeyed there [in the heavens] and trustworthy," (مُطاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ).

The use of "thamma" (ثَمَّ), which is used to point to something distant, refers to the fact that the messenger of Allah's revelation is influential and obeyed in the world of angels. Above all, he possesses the utmost trustworthiness in delivering his mission.

It is understood from narrations that sometimes Gabriel the Trustworthy was accompanied by a vast group of angels to deliver the verses of the Quran, and he was certainly obeyed among them. A messenger must be obeyed among his companions.

In a hadith, it is mentioned that when these verses were revealed, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to Gabriel:

"How beautifully your Lord has praised you!: Possessed of power, with the Lord of the Throne secure in rank, obeyed there and trustworthy. Tell me an example of your power and your trustworthiness!" Gabriel replied: "As for the example of my power, I was commanded to destroy the cities of the people of Lot; there were four cities, and in each city, there were four hundred thousand fighting men, besides their children. I uprooted these cities and took them up to the sky until the angels of heaven heard the sounds of their animals, then I brought them down and overturned them! As for the example of my trustworthiness, no command has ever been given to me from which I deviated in the slightest." [1]

Then, He proceeds to negate the false accusation attributed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), adding: "And your companion is not mad," (وَ ما صاحِبُكُمْ بِمَجْنُونٍ).

The use of the term "companion" (sahib), which means a constant associate and friend, besides reflecting the Prophet's humble station toward all people—showing he never intended to seek superiority—points to the fact that he lived among you for many years and was an associate of your individuals. You have known him for his intellect, wisdom, and trustworthiness; how then can you attribute madness to him?! It is only because, after his appointment, he brought teachings that do not align with your prejudices, blind imitations, and whims;

[1] Makin is from the root makanat, meaning "status and rank." As derived from the words of Raghib in Al-Mufradat and some other commentators, this root is originally a "noun of place" from the root k-w-n, then through frequent use, it was treated as a verbal root from which tamaskana was derived, like tamaskana from the root sukun. [1] Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 446. The same content is also mentioned in the commentary Al-Durr al-Manthur under the verses in question.

therefore, to excuse yourselves from obeying his commands, you cast such an unfair accusation upon him.

The accusation of "madness" is among the labels that, according to Quranic verses, were given to all divine prophets by stubborn and antagonistic enemies: كَذلِكَ ما أَتَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا قالُوا ساحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ ("Similarly, no messenger came to those before them but they said: 'A sorcerer or a madman'") (Adh-Dhariyat: 52).

In their logic, a "sane" person was one who conformed to the corrupt environment, followed their desires, lived for the moment, and avoided any reformist or revolutionary movement. By this standard and criterion, all prophets were "mad" from the dark perspective of world-worshippers.

Then, to emphasize the connection between the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and Gabriel the Trustworthy, He adds: "And he certainly saw him on the clear horizon," (وَ لَقَدْ رَآهُ بِالْأُفُقِ الْمُبِينِ). The "clear horizon" refers to the "highest horizon" and the horizon where angels are manifested, where the Prophet observed Gabriel.

Some have considered verse 7 of Surah An-Najm, which says: وَ هُوَ بِالْأُفُقِ الْأَعْلى‌ ("While he was on the highest horizon"), as evidence for this interpretation. However, as we explained in the commentary of Surah An-Najm, that verse, like other verses of that Surah, recounts another reality which will become clear upon referring to it.

Some have also said: The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) saw Gabriel in his original form twice: once at the beginning of his mission when Gabriel appeared to him on the upper horizon, covering the entire east and west, and he was of such greatness that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was deeply moved.

The second time was during the Ascension (Mi'raj), when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) saw him in the high heavens in his original form, and they consider the verse under discussion to refer to that.

There is also the possibility that it refers to the observation of Allah through inner spiritual vision. For further explanation in this regard, please refer to Volume 22, page 486 onwards (Surah An-Najm, verses 5 to 13).

Then He adds: "And he is not stingy with [the knowledge of] the unseen," (وَ ما هُوَ عَلَى الْغَيْبِ بِضَنِينٍ).

He places it all, without any reduction, at the disposal of Allah's servants. He is not like many people who, when they attain an important truth, insist on concealing it and are often stingy in expressing it, and perhaps take that knowledge with them to the grave.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is not like that; with total generosity, he places whatever he has received through revelation at the disposal of all who need it, and even at the disposal of those who do not value it, in the hope that they might be guided and take the path of truth.

"Dhanin" (ضنين) is from the root "dhinnah" (ضنة), meaning stinginess regarding precious and valuable things. This is a trait that never exists in prophets. If others possess such a trait due to their limited knowledge, the Prophet—whose source of knowledge is the boundless ocean of Allah's knowledge—is free from such attributes.

And He also adds: "And it is not the word of an accursed devil," (وَ ما هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَيْطانٍ رَجِيمٍ).

These Quranic verses are never like the words of soothsayers received through connection with devils, and the signs of this truth are apparent in them. For the words of soothsayers were mixed with lies and many errors and revolved around their desires and greed; this has no relation to the Holy Quran.

In reality, this is a response to another of the slanders of the polytheists who said the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) was a soothsayer and that what he brought was taken from devils. While the word of the devil is dark and misleading, the Quranic verses—as is clear to anyone upon first encounter—are entirely light, guidance, and illumination.

The word "rajim" (رجيم) is originally from the root "rajm" (رجم), meaning stone, and was then applied to throwing stones to drive away a person or animal. Subsequently, it came to mean any kind of expulsion or distancing. The meaning of "shaitan rajim" here is this: the devil who is expelled from the presence of Allah.

Key Points Conditions of a Worthy Messenger

The five attributes mentioned in the above verses for "Gabriel the Trustworthy" as Allah's messenger to the noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) are attributes that, considering the hierarchy, are necessary for every messenger.

First is nobility (karamat) and possessing outstanding internal qualities that make him worthy of an important mission.

Then, possessing power (dhi quwwah) to proceed in his mission with decisiveness and strength, and to be free from any weakness or lethargy.

Then, having status and rank in proximity to the one from whom he has accepted the mission (makin), so that he can receive the messages well and, if a response is needed, deliver it without any fear or hesitation.

In the fourth stage, if the mission is important, he must have assistants to help him in this task—assistants who are obedient, coordinated, and submissive (muta').

And in the final stage, possessing "trustworthiness" (amanat) so that those who wish to receive the message from that messenger can trust him, and his words are considered, without addition or subtraction, the words of the one from whom he came.

When these five principles are secured, the right of the mission will be fulfilled. Therefore, in the states of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his life history, we see that he chose his messengers with great care from among those who possessed these attributes. A vivid example of this is the mission of Amir al-Mu'minin Ali (عليه السلام) on behalf of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to deliver the opening verses of Surah Al-Bara'ah to the polytheists of Mecca under those specific conditions, the details of which were mentioned in the commentary of Surah Al-Bara'ah.

Ali (عليه السلام) says:

رسولك ترجمان عقلك و كتابك ابلغ ما ينطق عنك

"Your messenger is the interpreter of your intellect, and your letter is the most eloquent speaker on your behalf." [1]

[1] Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 301.

Ayahs

فَأَيْنَ تَذْهَبُونَ (٢٦)
So where are you going? (26)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٧)
It is just a reminder for all the nations, (27)
لِمَن شَآءَ مِنكُمْ أَن يَسْتَقِيمَ (٢٨)
for those of you who wish to walk straight; (28)
وَمَا تَشَآءُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٩)
but you will not wish unless it is wished by Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (29)

Commentary

O Heedless Ones, Where Are You Going?!

In the preceding verses, this reality was clarified: that the Holy Quran is the Word of Allah, for its content demonstrates that it is not satanic speech but rather Divine discourse. It was revealed through the Messenger of Allah’s revelation with complete power and trustworthiness upon a Prophet who possesses the utmost intellectual balance—a Prophet who never withheld anything in conveying his mission and expressed what was taught to him without any addition or omission.

In the verses under discussion, He rebukes the opponents for their failure to follow this great Word. Using a reproachful interrogation, He says: "With all this, where then are you going?" (فَأَيْنَ تَذْهَبُونَ).

Why have you abandoned the straight path and stepped into the wilderness? Why have you turned your backs on this blazing lamp and headed toward darkness? Are you the enemies of your own prosperity and well-being?

Then He adds: "This [Quran] is nothing but a reminder for all the worlds" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ). It counsels everyone and warns everyone, so they may awaken from the sleep of heedlessness.

And since the "action of the Actor" alone is not sufficient for guidance and education, but the "receptivity of the recipient" is also a condition, He adds in the next verse: "The Quran is a source of awakening for those among you who will to follow the straight path" (لِمَنْ شاءَ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ يَسْتَقِيمَ).

It is noteworthy that in the previous verse He says: "The Quran is a source of remembrance and awakening for all the worlds," yet in this verse, He mentions only a specific group—those who have made a decision to accept guidance and tread the straight path. This distinction is because the previous verse expresses the universality of this Divine grace, while this verse states the condition for benefiting from this grace. All blessings in the world are such that the essence of the grace is general, but its utilization is conditional upon will and decision.

A similar meaning appears in verse 2 of Surah Al-Baqarah: ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ: "This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those conscious of Allah."

In any case, this is one of the verses that demonstrates Allah has created man free and possessed of volition, and the final decision to tread the path of truth or falsehood rests with him.

The expression "yastaqim" (to be straight/upright) is a beautiful expression showing that the primary path set before man is the straight path of guidance and prosperity, and all other paths are deviations. All internal and external forces of man are mobilized to move him along this straight path; if it were not for excesses, deficiencies, satanic whisperings, and misleading propaganda, man would step into this very straight path by the call of his primordial nature (fitrah). We know that a straight line is always the shortest path to the destination.

However, since the expression regarding man's will and volition might create the illusion that man is so free that he has no need for Allah or Divine success (tawfiq) in treading this path, the next verse—which is the final verse of this Surah—explains the influence of the Lord's will, saying: "And you do not will except that Allah wills—the Lord of the worlds" (وَ ما تَشاؤُنَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ).

In reality, the combination of these two verses expresses that precise and subtle matter of "a position between two positions" (amr bayn al-amrayn). On one hand, it says the decision-making is in your hands; on the other hand, it says: unless Allah wills, you cannot decide. That is, if you are created with choice and freedom, this choice and freedom are also from Allah's side; He has willed for you to be so.

Man is neither compelled in his actions nor one hundred percent independent. Neither the way of "compulsion" (jabr) is correct, nor the way of "absolute delegation" (tafwid). Rather, whatever he possesses—from intellect, intelligence, physical ability, and the power of decision-making—is all from Allah's side. It is this very reality that, on one hand, makes him constantly in need of the Creator and, on the other hand, gives him commitment and responsibility by virtue of his freedom and choice.

The expression "Lord of the Worlds" (رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ) clearly shows that the Divine will is also in the direction of the education and perfection of man and all the worlds. He never wants anyone to be led astray, to sin, or to fall far from the proximity of Allah. By virtue of His Lordship, He assists all those who wish to step into the path of perfection.

It is strange that the proponents of the school of compulsion have clung only to the second verse, while the proponents of absolute delegation might resort to the first verse. This separation of verses from one another—which is often the result of incorrect preconceptions—is a source of deviation. The verses of the Quran must be placed side by side, and the conclusion drawn from the whole.

Interestingly, some commentators have narrated that when the first verse (لِمَنْ شاءَ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ يَسْتَقِيمَ) was revealed, Abu Jahl, who was practically a proponent of the belief in absolute delegation, said: "It is well that all authority is with us." It was then that the second verse was revealed: وَ ما تَشاؤُنَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ [1].

O Allah! We know that treading the path of truth is not possible except through Your success; grant us success in treading this path. O Lord! We have desired to step into the path of guidance; You also will to take our hands on this path.

O God! The scene of the Gathering and Your court of justice is very terrifying, and our record of deeds is empty of good works; place us in the shelter of Your forgiveness and grace, not before the scale of justice. Amin, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Al-Takwir

[1]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," vol. 30, p. 62, and "Ruh al-Bayan," vol. 10, p. 354.
